reviewed_map.rationale: These startups provide dedicated platforms for automated scoring, dataset creation, regression tracking, and LLM-as-judge evaluation, enabling teams to measure and continuously improve LLM reliability.
citations: Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation

reviewed_map.rationale: They deliver tracing, real‑time monitoring, and the ability to convert evaluation results into production guardrails, ensuring continuous observability of LLM applications.
citations: Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation

reviewed_map.rationale: These companies offer vector storage, retrieval pipelines, and memory services that underpin retrieval‑augmented generation and ensure high‑quality context for LLMs.
Provides scalable, isolated sandboxes for running LLM‑generated code, supporting massive concurrency and safe execution for AI‑first applications.

retrieved_docs.title: Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation

Langfuse is an open-source platform for tracing, evaluating, and monitoring LLM applications. It captures full traces of agent runs including prompts, completions, tool calls, and latency. Langfuse integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and OpenAI SDK, and supports human annotation workflows for building evaluation datasets. It is used by teams to detect regressions, measure hallucination rates, and optimize prompt quality.

retrieved_docs.title: Braintrust: Enterprise LLM Evaluation Platform
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Braintrust: Enterprise LLM Evaluation Platform

Braintrust is an enterprise-grade evaluation platform for LLM applications that supports automated scoring, human review workflows, and regression tracking across model versions. Teams use Braintrust to define evaluation datasets, run scoring functions (LLM-as-judge, exact match, semantic similarity), and track quality metrics over time. It competes with Langfuse in the evaluation-native tooling space.

retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: Weaviate Blog — Concepts
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Weaviate Blog — Concepts

Your LLM Is Only as Good as What It Retrieves
A Researcher's Perspective on Retrieval Quality in RAG Systems
A Researcher's Perspective on Retrieval Quality in RAG Systems
A deep dive into Engram, our managed memory service for agents which is simple to get started but adaptable to any use case.
Two weeks of dogfooding Engram, Weaviate's memory product, in daily Claude Code sessions. This surfaced where a dedicated memory product adds value, and the specific mechanics that prevent integration with coding assistants from working well.
Memory isn't just a feature for AI applications—it's infrastructure. As agents scale, the limited loop of stateless interactions breaks down, and continuity becomes a systems problem that requires active maintenance.
Context engineering is how AI agents manage LLM memory—selecting, retrieving, and organizing context from short-term and long-term memory to improve reliability in production.
Hands-on patterns: Design pattern for gen-AI enterprise applications, with Arize AI.
A strategic guide to the costs, risks and rewards of upgrading embedding models in production AI

retrieved_docs.title: Langfuse — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Langfuse — about

About us
Langfuse is building the open source LLM engineering platform to help teams build production-grade LLM applications faster (Why Langfuse?).
We are excited about the potential of LLMs and generative AI and the impacts it will have on all of us. Doing our part in accelerating this shift is our mission.
We initially realized the pains of solving problems with LLMs during Y Combinator's W23 batch in San Francisco. We now have two offices, a larger one in Berlin, Germany (with a focus on product & engineering) and a smaller one in San Francisco (with a focus on marketing & sales). We hire product & engineering remotely across Europe, with a week onsite per month in Berlin.
In January 2026, we joined ClickHouse to accelerate even further. Learn more about our story and mission.
Team
Backed by
We were backed by a fantastic group of investors:
- Lightspeed Venture Partners – why Alex partnered with us
- General Catalyst
- Y Combinator
Now, we're part of Clickhouse, the leading open-source database for real-time analytics - why we joined Clickhouse

retrieved_docs.title: Galileo — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Galileo — about

Building the Future of Reliable AI
AI systems will reinvent how work gets done, but non-deterministic LLMs introduce risk. The key to success is reliability. Teams need a new workflow that puts reliability at the heart of AI initiatives.
Driven to innovate
At Galileo, we believe that AI can be trustworthy, safe, and observable. We're helping builders create that future with workflows that span evaluation, monitoring, observability, and real-time guardrails. AI systems will only grow in complexity, and our mission is to sift through all the signals to help developers fix unexpected behaviors.
AI applications continue to change quickly. LLMs advance every week, and so do user expectations. It's hard to know if your app is ready for tomorrow. The key to success is continuous improvement, blocking mistakes in production and then immediately bringing that learning back into the development cycle. By bringing the power of evaluation to the whole development lifecycle, Galileo delivers a loop of fast feedback, so you can drive rapid improvement based on real user experiences.
There's no faster way to ship reliable AI applications.
Leadership
Galileo was founded in 2021 to pursue a vision

retrieved_docs.title: Cresta Blog — cresta-named-one-of-americas-best-startup-employers-for-the-third-year-in-a-row
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Cresta Blog — cresta-named-one-of-americas-best-startup-employers-for-the-third-year-in-a-row

We’re proud to share that Cresta has been named to Forbes’ list of America’s Best Startup Employers 2026 — and this year, we’ve climbed to #58 out of 500 companies nationwide.
This marks our third consecutive year on the list. And each year, we’ve moved up.
Breaking into the top 100 startup employers in the country is an incredible milestone, and one that reflects something far more meaningful than a number: the strength of our team, our culture, and the impact we’re building together.
What This Recognition Means
The Forbes list, produced in partnership with Statista, evaluates more than 20,000 eligible startups across the U.S., narrowing them down to 2,700 for detailed analysis and ultimately selecting just 500 companies for the final ranking.
Rankings are determined using more than 7 million data points, with companies evaluated across three core pillars:
- Employer Reputation
- Employee Satisfaction
- Company Growth
The scoring model combines social listening, employee review data, and measurable growth indicators like headcount, hiring momentum, and web traffic.
In other words, this isn’t a popularity contest. It’s a data-driven assessment of which startups are building strong,

retrieved_docs.title: Galileo — www.rungalileo.io
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Galileo — www.rungalileo.io

Don't just monitor AI failures. Stop them.
Galileo is the AI observability and eval engineering platform where offline evals become production guardrails.
Capture your groundtruth
Build your datasets from synthetic, development, and live production data. Capture subject matter expert annotations to create a living asset that continuously grounds your AI systems.
Build accurate evals
Don't settle for generic evals with less than 70% F1 scores. Galileo auto-tunes metrics from live feedback to create evals that are fit to your environments.
Go from evals to guardrails
Today’s evals are tomorrow’s guardrails. But only if you can run them at scale. Distill your optimized evals into Luna models that monitor 100% of your traffic at 97% lower cost.
Solve the AI measurement problem
You can't ship when you're flying blind. Start with 20+ out-of-box evals for RAG, agents, safety, and security—then build the custom evaluators to encode your domain expertise. Only Galileo distills expensive LLM-as-judge evaluators into compact Luna models that run with low-latency and low-cost.
Accelerate your deployments
Developers need to know what to fix. So Galileo's insights engine analyzes agent behavior

retrieved_docs.title: Modal Blog — lovable-case-study
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Modal Blog — lovable-case-study

How Modal powered 250,000 Lovable app creations in a weekend
Lovable is one of the fastest growing startups in history, having hit $75M ARR a mere 7 months after they launched. Lovable is on a mission to build the “last piece of software,” enabling anyone to create fully functional applications without writing code. Users enter a prompt describing the app they want, and Lovable generates the code, spins up the application, and enables real-time visual edits. Lovable uses Modal Sandboxes at massive scale to run LLM-generated code for thousands of apps in safe and isolated sandboxes.
The problem: performance and reliability at scale
In June 2025, Lovable had a major promotional weekend event coming up, in collaboration with Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google. This event was set to dramatically increase user activity.
Unfortunately, Lovable’s existing code sandbox provider—a distributed cloud VM platform—raised concerns around scalability and operational risk. The team wasn’t confident the provider could scale fast enough to meet the expected demand, and with only this single vendor in place, Lovable was exposed to high risk of service disruption.
